title: B1 Preliminary — Use of English
description: Test sprawdzający znajomość angielskiego na poziomie B1

questions.text: She ___ to school every day by bus.
questions.explanation: Third person singular (she/he/it) in present simple takes the -s/-es ending.

questions.text: When I arrived at the party, most people ___ already left.
questions.explanation: Past perfect (had + past participle) is used for an action completed before another past action.

questions.text: This book is ___ than the one I read last week.
questions.explanation: For adjectives with 3+ syllables, use 'more + adjective' for the comparative form.

questions.text: You ___ wear a seatbelt in a car — it's the law.
questions.explanation: 'Must' expresses strong obligation or necessity, especially from an external rule or law.

questions.text: She is really good ___ playing the piano.
questions.explanation: The fixed expression is 'good at (doing) something'.

questions.text: I couldn't find ___ information about the event online.
questions.explanation: 'Any' is used in negative sentences and questions with uncountable nouns like 'information'.

questions.text: He ___ up late every night this week.
questions.explanation: Present perfect continuous (has/have been + -ing) describes an ongoing action over a recent period.

questions.text: Please turn ___ the lights before you leave.
questions.explanation: 'Turn off' means to stop an electrical device from working.

questions.text: ___ Eiffel Tower is in Paris.
questions.explanation: The definite article 'the' is used with unique landmarks and famous structures.

questions.text: She asked me ___ I had finished my homework.
questions.explanation: 'If' is used in reported questions when the direct question is a yes/no question.

questions.text: We arrived ___ the airport two hours before the flight.
questions.explanation: 'At' is used with specific locations like airports, stations, and stops.

questions.text: If it ___ tomorrow, we will cancel the picnic.
questions.explanation: In first conditional sentences, the if-clause uses present simple (not will).

questions.text: He likes football ___ he doesn't like basketball.
questions.explanation: 'But' is used to express contrast between two ideas.

questions.text: She ___ for this company since 2019.
questions.explanation: Present perfect (has/have + past participle) is used with 'since' to describe an action continuing from the past to now.

questions.text: My sister is ___ than me — she always gets top marks.
questions.explanation: Short adjectives (one or two syllables) form the comparative by adding -er.

questions.text: Can you look ___ my dog while I'm away?
questions.explanation: 'Look after' means to take care of someone or something.

questions.text: I have never ___ sushi before.
questions.explanation: Present perfect uses 'have/has + past participle'. The past participle of 'eat' is 'eaten'.

questions.text: The train ___ at 7:30 every morning.
questions.explanation: Present simple is used for timetabled or scheduled events. Third person singular takes -s/-es.

questions.text: He decided to give ___ smoking for health reasons.
questions.explanation: 'Give up' means to stop doing something, especially a habit.

questions.text: I was born ___ the 5th of March.
questions.explanation: 'On' is used with specific dates.

questions.text: The new shopping centre is very ___ for people who live nearby.
questions.explanation: CONVENIENCE (noun) → convenient (adjective): the -ce ending is replaced by -t to form the adjective.

questions.text: She showed great ___ when she helped the injured man.
questions.explanation: KIND (adjective) → kindness (noun): add the suffix -ness to form an abstract noun.

questions.text: He was ___ about passing the exam.
questions.explanation: HOPE (noun/verb) → hopeful (adjective): add -ful to form an adjective meaning 'full of hope'.

questions.text: The teacher spoke very ___ to the nervous student.
questions.explanation: GENTLE (adjective) → gently (adverb): drop the final -e and add -ly.

questions.text: The film was so ___ that I fell asleep.
questions.explanation: BORE (verb) → boring (adjective): add -ing to describe something that causes boredom.

questions.text: The instructions were very ___ and easy to follow.
questions.explanation: CLEAR (adjective) → clearly (adverb): add -ly to form an adverb describing how something is done.

questions.text: Their ___ of the project took three months.
questions.explanation: COMPLETE (verb) → completion (noun): drop -e and add -ion to form a noun.

questions.text: She was very ___ when she heard the bad news.
questions.explanation: HAPPY (adjective) → unhappy (adjective): add prefix un- to create the opposite meaning.

questions.text: The weather was ___ and we had a great time outdoors.
questions.explanation: SUN (noun) → sunny (adjective): double the final consonant and add -y.

questions.text: There was a lot of ___ at the concert.
questions.explanation: EXCITE (verb) → excitement (noun): drop -e and add -ment to form a noun.
